title: DORA ICT Risk Management Dashboard
exclusions: ICT risks formally accepted through the management body's risk appetite framework with documented compensating controls and time-bounded review dates; risks from systems scheduled for decommissioning within 90 days.
evidence: ICT risk dashboard exports showing risk inventory, scoring methodology, residual scores, mitigation status, and management body acceptance decisions archived quarterly for competent authority requests.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a test ICT risk scoring event with a risk level exceeding the risk appetite threshold (e.g., residual score 9.5/10); verify the dashboard updates within 15 minutes and generates an escalation alert to the risk committee with risk ID, owner, and score.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Inject a test ICT risk scoring event with a residual score within appetite (e.g., 3/10); verify no escalation alert fires and the risk appears correctly categorised in the dashboard as within tolerance.

app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621)
spl:
index=risk sourcetype="stash" earliest=-30d@d
| stats latest(risk_score) as residual_risk, max(risk_score) as peak_risk, dc(source) as contributing_sources, values(source) as source_list by risk_object, risk_object_type
| lookup business_entity_lookup risk_object OUTPUT business_entity
| fillnull value="UNASSIGNED" business_entity
| stats avg(residual_risk) as avg_residual, max(residual_risk) as max_residual, sum(contributing_sources) as total_sources by business_entity
| sort - avg_residual
| table business_entity, avg_residual, max_residual, total_sources
description: Produces an auditable, continuously refreshed view of residual ICT risk by business entity using the ES risk scoring pipeline, so risk owners can evidence identification, assessment, and monitoring of ICT risk without manual spreadsheet rollups.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for DORA compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes risk and sourcetypes stash. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Ensure ES Risk Notable / risk scoring populates `index=risk`; (2) create KV lookup `business_entity_lookup` keyed by `risk_object` (hosts/users/identities) mapping to `business_entity` from CMDB/ServiceNow export; (3) schedule daily for management reporting; (4) drill down to `risk_object` detail in Dashboard Studio. Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=stash`.
visualization: Bar chart (avg/max residual risk by entity), Single value KPI tiles (top entity risk), Table with drilldown. Key fields: `avg_residual`, `business_entity`, `contributing_sources`, `max_residual`, `peak_risk`, `residual_risk`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Planned risk acceptance decisions** — ICT risks formally accepted through the management body's risk appetite framework with documented compensating controls appear as open items in the risk dashboard but have appropriate governance approval per Art.5(2).

2. **Risk scoring methodology transitions** — Changes between risk quantification frameworks (e.g., transitioning from qualitative to quantitative scoring) may create apparent risk level jumps without actual risk posture changes.

3. **Inherited risks from recent acquisitions** — ICT risks from newly acquired entities integrated into the risk register may show elevated totals before the integration risk mitigation plan completes.

4. **Residual risk from approved technology debt** — Legacy systems scheduled for replacement within the approved technology roadmap carry documented residual risk that is tracked but time-bounded.

5. **Cross-entity consolidation effects** — Group-level risk dashboards aggregating subsidiary risks may double-count shared infrastructure risks before deduplication normalisation completes.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`dora_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly per the ICT risk management framework review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `risk` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `business_entity_lookup` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('business_entity_lookup')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.5`. The control objective is: "Evidence that DORA Art.5 (ICT risk-management governance) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.3.1: DORA ICT Risk Management Dashboard." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `risk` under `stash`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Ensure ES Risk Notable / risk scoring populates `index=risk`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** create KV lookup `business_entity_lookup` keyed by `risk_object` (hosts/users/identities) mapping to `business_entity` from CMDB/ServiceNow export. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 3:** schedule daily for management reporting. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 4:** drill down to `risk_object` detail in Dashboard Studio. Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=stash`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "DORA ICT Risk Management Dashboard," the aggregation produces the risk posture — the per-row values should reconcile to the organisation's risk register entries, not stand alone as engineering metrics.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `business_entity_lookup` — the enrichment table that adds compliance-relevant business context to raw events. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`business_entity`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `risk_object`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `business_entity_lookup` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval risk_object = lower(risk_object)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 30 days.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.3.1: DORA ICT Risk Management Dashboard]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.3.1,reg=DORA
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=risk sourcetype=stash earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `avg_residual`, `business_entity`, `contributing_sources`, `max_residual`, `peak_risk`, `residual_risk`, `risk_object`, `risk_object_type`, `source_list`, `total_sources`. Run:

```spl
index=risk sourcetype=stash earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table avg_residual, business_entity, contributing_sources, max_residual, peak_risk, residual_risk, risk_object, risk_object_type
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `business_entity_lookup`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup business_entity_lookup
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.3.1: DORA evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Distribution (`avg`, `max residual risk by entity`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 2 — Single Value Kpi Tiles (`top entity risk`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Table With Drilldown (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**DORA-specific failure modes:**

• Third-party ICT risk register (Art. 28): DORA requires a Register of Information listing every critical ICT third-party service provider. Detections touching supplier risk must reference this register; if a supplier appears in telemetry but not in the Register, that is a Register-completeness finding, not just a technical anomaly.

• Testing cadence (Art. 24-27): DORA's TLPT (Threat-Led Penetration Testing) is required every 3 years for significant institutions. Detections measuring resilience testing must differentiate TLPT (supervisor-led) from self-assessment — mixing them dilutes the evidence.

• Incident classification thresholds (Art. 18): DORA uses specific criteria for 'major' vs 'significant' incidents — client impact, duration, data volume. Detections must classify against these specific thresholds, which differ from GDPR breach criteria; a single event can be major-DORA but not a personal-data breach.

• Resilience testing record retention (Art. 27): results of digital operational resilience tests must be retained. Align log retention to match — typically 5+ years. Verify `frozenTimePeriodInSecs >= 157680000` on any index named by a DORA resilience-testing UC.
grandmaExplanation: We stay on top of ict risk management dashboard — so we can show we govern technology and resilience risk the way financial rules that apply to us require.
